statement.markdown:
You are given the root node of a binary search tree (BST) and a value to insert into the tree. Return the root node of the BST after the insertion. It is guaranteed that the value does not exist in the tree.

Note: The number of nodes in the BST will be in the range [1, 10^4].
The values of each node in the BST will be in the range [1, 10^8].
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains the number of nodes in the BST.
The next lines contain the node values, one per line, representing the in-order traversal of the BST.
The last line contains the value to be inserted.
statement.outputFormat:
The first line contains the number of nodes in the updated BST.
The next lines contain the node values, one per line, representing the in-order traversal of the updated BST.

constraints.variable: Number of nodes in the BST
constraints.description: The number of nodes in the BST
The values of each node in the BST

hints.text: To insert a node into a BST, you should start by understanding the properties of a BST. A BST is a tree in which for every node, the values in the left child are less than the node's value and the values in the right child are greater than the node's value.

The insertion process in a BST involves traversing the tree from the root node down to the correct location for the new node. This process can be implemented recursively or iteratively. Think about how you can use the BST properties to guide the traversal.

A key part of the solution involves finding the correct parent node for the new value. This means comparing the new value with the current node's value and deciding whether to go left or right. Consider how you can use a loop or recursive calls to handle this comparison at each node.

editorial.title: Insert Node in BST
editorial.intuition: The problem requires inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST) while maintaining the BST property. A key observation is that in a BST, all nodes to the left of a node have values less than the node, and all nodes to the right have values greater. This property can be leveraged to find the correct position for the new node.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the entire tree for each insertion, checking if the current node is the correct location for the new node. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2) for n insertions, making it inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to utilize the BST property to directly find the parent node for the new node. This can be achieved by starting at the root and iteratively moving left or right based on the comparison of the new node's value with the current node's value.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves a iterative or recursive approach that directly finds the correct position for the new node in O(h) time, where h is the height of the tree. For a balanced BST, this translates to O(log n) time. However, in the worst case (an unbalanced tree), it could be O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the algorithm maintains the BST property. For any given node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater. When inserting a new node, the algorithm traverses the tree in a way that ensures the new node will be in the correct position relative to its ancestors, thus preserving the BST property.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs, such as inserting nodes with values 5, 3, 7, 2, 4, 6, 8 into an initially empty BST and then inserting a target value of 9, helps verify that the solution produces the expected output and correctly maintains the BST structure.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle the case where the tree is empty.
Incorrectly updating the child pointers of the parent node.
Not properly traversing the tree to find the insertion point.
Forgetting to return the root of the updated tree.
Not considering the time and space complexity implications of different approaches.

functionSignature.java: public TreeNode insertIntoBST(TreeNode root, int val);
functionSignature.python: def insertIntoBST(self, root: TreeNode, val: int) -> TreeNode:
